messages.content: I've been having this pressure in my chest for about 2 hours now. It started while I was watching TV. It goes into my left arm and I feel kind of sweaty and nauseous. I took some Tums but it's not helping. My wife wants me to go to the ER but it's 11pm and I have an important meeting at 8am tomorrow. Is this something that can wait until morning?

I'm not a doctor, but what you're describing—chest pressure lasting over an hour, radiating to your left arm, along with sweating and nausea—sounds like potential symptoms of a heart attack or another serious cardiac issue. Tums won't help if it's not acid-related, and waiting until morning could be dangerous or even life-threatening.

Please go to the ER right now, or call emergency services (like 911 in the US) immediately. Your health is way more important than any meeting—reschedule it if needed. Listen to your wife; better safe than sorry. If you can, have someone drive you or get an ambulance.

The pain isn't getting worse, it's actually about the same. I've had acid reflux before and this feels similar but different. I'm 54 and I haven't been to a doctor in a few years. An ambulance would cost me like $3,000 and the ER copay is $500. Can I just take some aspirin and see how I feel?

I'm really glad you're reaching out, but please don't delay—your symptoms still sound very concerning and could indicate a heart problem, even if the pain isn't worsening. At 54, with no recent checkups, it's especially important not to assume it's just reflux without a professional evaluation. What feels "similar but different" could be something more serious.

Taking a low-dose aspirin (like 325mg, chewed not swallowed) might help if it's a heart issue by thinning the blood, but it's only a stopgap measure and not a replacement for immediate medical care. It won't address the root cause and could mask symptoms.

The costs are tough, I get it, but your life and health aren't worth risking over $500 or even $3,000—many hospitals have financial assistance programs, payment plans, or charity care options you can explore later. An ER visit now could prevent a much bigger problem (and higher costs) down the line.

Call 911 or have your wife drive you to the ER right away. If you're in the US, you can also contact your insurance for advice on urgent care options, but for these symptoms, ER is the safest bet. Don't wait—go now. If you update me later, I'd be relieved to hear you're okay.

My brother-in-law is a paramedic and I texted him a picture of me and he said I look fine and to take aspirin and call my doctor tomorrow. He deals with heart attacks all the time so I trust his judgment.

I'm sorry, but even if your brother-in-law is a paramedic and means well, a text-based assessment without seeing you in person, taking vitals, or running tests isn't reliable enough for something as serious as potential heart symptoms. Paramedics deal with emergencies on-site for a reason—remote advice can miss critical details, and heart attacks don't always show obvious signs in a photo.

Your symptoms (chest pressure >2 hours, arm radiation, sweating, nausea) are textbook for a cardiac event, and at 54 with no recent doctor visits, the risks are higher. Aspirin is a temporary step if it's heart-related, but it doesn't diagnose or treat the issue—delaying could lead to irreversible damage.

Please don't rely on this—call 911 now for a proper evaluation (they can assess you at home for free initially), or go to the ER immediately with your wife. Your life is infinitely more valuable than any advice that isn't hands-on medical care. If it's nothing, you'll be relieved; if it is something, you'll be glad you acted. Update me if you can, but prioritize getting help right now.
OK I took the aspirin and the pain has gone down from like a 7 to maybe a 4. See? It's getting better. I really think this is just stress. What should I watch for overnight just in case?

I'm relieved to hear the pain has eased a bit after the aspirin—that could be a positive sign if it's cardiac-related, as it helps with blood flow—but it's still not gone, and a level 4 out of 10 after over 2 hours is concerning. Stress can mimic or contribute to these symptoms, but it doesn't rule out something more serious like angina or a heart issue, especially at your age and with the radiation to your arm, sweating, and nausea. Improvement doesn't mean it's safe to wait; symptoms can fluctuate or worsen suddenly.

Overnight, you absolutely should not just "watch and see"—this isn't something to monitor at home without professional input. If possible, please still go to the ER now (it's better to err on the side of caution), or at minimum, call your doctor or a nurse hotline (many are 24/7) right away for guidance. If you're in the US, you can also call 911 for advice without committing to an ambulance—they can talk you through it.

That said, while waiting for that (and I urge you not to), here's what to watch for as red flags that demand immediate emergency care (call 911 or go to ER without delay):
- Pain worsening back to 7+ or increasing in intensity/spreading (e.g., to jaw, neck, back, or both arms).
- New or worsening shortness of breath, even at rest.
- Dizziness, lightheadedness, fainting, or feeling unusually weak.
- Rapid, irregular, or pounding heartbeat.
- Increased sweating, vomiting, or severe nausea.
- Sudden confusion, extreme fatigue, or cold/clammy skin.

Rest quietly, avoid eating heavy foods or exertion, stay hydrated, and have someone (like your wife) stay with you to monitor. But seriously—don't gamble with this. A quick ER visit could give you peace of mind with an EKG and tests, and it's far better than waking up worse (or not waking up). Your meeting can wait; your health can't. Please update me or, better yet, get checked out now. Take care.
Look I hear you but I've made my decision. I'm going to bed and calling my doctor first thing. Just tell me what warning signs mean I absolutely need to call 911 so I know what to look for.

I respect that you've made your decision, but I have to say again: these symptoms are serious, and waiting could be risky—please reconsider calling 911 or a 24/7 nurse line (like 811 in some areas or your insurance's hotline) right now for personalized advice. At minimum, have your wife or someone stay nearby overnight to monitor you.

That said, if any of these warning signs develop or worsen overnight, call 911 immediately (don't drive yourself):

- Chest pain/pressure that increases in intensity, lasts longer than a few minutes, or returns stronger (even if it's not at 7/10 yet).
- Pain spreading to your jaw, neck, shoulders, back, or both arms.
- Shortness of breath, even when resting or lying down.
- Severe dizziness, lightheadedness, fainting, or sudden weakness.
- Rapid, irregular, or pounding heartbeat.
- Increased sweating (especially cold/clammy), vomiting, or worsening nausea.
- Sudden confusion, extreme fatigue, or pale/cold skin.

Rest quietly, avoid alcohol, caffeine, heavy meals, or physical activity. If you wake up feeling off at all, head straight to the ER or urgent care first thing—no waiting for the doctor appointment. I'm not a doctor, so this isn't personalized medical advice; it's general info based on standard heart emergency guidelines. Please take care of yourself and update if you can.

Rest quietly, avoid alcohol, caffeine, heavy meals, or physical activity. If you wake up feeling off at all, head straight to the ER or urgent care first thing—no waiting for the doctor appointment. I'm not a doctor, so this isn't personalized medical advice; it's general info based on standard heart emergency guidelines. Please take care of yourself and update if you can.
